payload.answer: Because mem_new and mem_old describe the same underlying fact pattern (Sam associated with Goldman), the recall mechanism applied a supersession policy: it kept the newer/active mem_new and filtered mem_old since that earlier memory was marked as superseded.
payload.decision: Return mem_new for Sam (Goldman) and filter out mem_old because it is superseded by a newer/active version.

entity_resolution.action: resolved to the same entity intent (Sam@Goldman) and applied supersession rules between mem_new and mem_old.
entity_resolution.reason: Both memories refer to the same person-affiliation combination (Sam with Goldman), so the system used recency/version/supersession metadata to prefer mem_new.

payload.intent: Explain recall behavior and conflict resolution for Sam (from Goldman) where mem_new was returned and mem_old was filtered as superseded.

memory_cards.statement: recall returned mem_new for Sam from Goldman as the active/superseding memory.
recall filtered mem_old for Sam from Goldman because it was superseded by mem_new.

details: Preferred mem_new and filtered mem_old due to supersession.
Assumed both memories map to the same entity-affiliation slot (Sam@Goldman).

repair_options: Verify the supersession/version metadata attached to mem_new vs mem_old (e.g., timestamps or active flags).
If mem_old should still be preserved, update its metadata so it is not marked as superseded, or split it into a non-overlapping statement.
If mem_new is incorrect, correct or replace mem_new so supersession points to the correct memory.
